name: Rubber Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Rubber. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for rubber cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength for optimal rubber absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise rubber removal with adequate cleaning area

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient rubber cleaning with good process control

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective rubber ablation while preserving substrate integrity

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective rubber ablation with controlled thermal effects

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform rubber removal without excessive heat accumulation

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete rubber removal with process control

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform rubber removal without excessive processing time

power.description: Low power to prevent thermal degradation of rubber (low melting point ~100-200°C); typical range from industrial fiber lasers for polymers. Recommended start at 50W for light contamination. Source: IPG Photonics specs for cleaning delicate substrates; academic review in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on polymer ablation.

scan_speed.description: High speeds minimize heat accumulation on low-thermal-conductivity rubber; slower for heavy contamination (1000-3000 mm/s), faster for light (5000-10000 mm/s). Recommended 5000 mm/s for balance. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2020) for surface prep.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective industrial cleaning; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-minimal HAZ but less common for rubber. 20 ns balances ablation of contaminants with low heat input. Source: Laser physics in Applied Surface Science (2019) on polymer cleaning thresholds; ns pulses avoid melting unlike longer durations.

repetition_rate.description: Moderate rates for effective contaminant removal without excessive heat buildup in rubber; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk accumulation. Trade-off noted in literature. Source: Academic literature in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021); typical for 1064 nm fiber lasers from Coherent specs.

energy_density.description: Low fluence to stay below rubber damage threshold (~0.5-1 J/cm² for ablation onset); 0.1-0.5 J/cm² for light contaminants, up to 1 J/cm² for heavy. Conservative for safe cleaning. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017) on elastomers; uncertainty noted for specific rubber types due to variability in composition.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots for precise cleaning on rubber surfaces; 100-200 μm for fine detail (e.g., molds), larger for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform coverage without overlap issues. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and Adapt Laser Systems; process optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2022).

pass_count.description: Multi-pass for thorough removal of stubborn contaminants on rubber; single pass for light soiling, 2-3 for typical industrial prep. Recommended 2 passes to ensure completeness without damage. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Rubber & Plastics News (2020); multi-pass approaches in laser cleaning guidelines from SPIE proceedings.

overlap_ratio.description: 50% overlap ensures uniform coverage and contaminant removal on irregular rubber surfaces; lower (30%) for speed, higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination. Balances efficiency and evenness. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics and Laser Technology (2019); typical range from manufacturer guidelines like those from CleanLaser.

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural properties affecting cleaning outcomes and material integrity

laserDamageThreshold.source_name: Laser-induced damage threshold of rubber composites for cleaning applications
laserDamageThreshold.citation: K. Srinivasan et al., Journal of Applied Physics, 2018, DOI: 10.1063/1.5028374
laserDamageThreshold.context: Vulcanized natural rubber composite with 30% carbon black filler, 25°C, measured using 1064 nm Nd:YAG pulsed laser (10 ns pulse duration) under vacuum conditions

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds

ablationThreshold.source_name: Laser Ablation of Elastomeric Composites: Threshold Fluence for Rubber Materials
ablationThreshold.citation: Dupont, A. et al., Journal of Applied Polymer Science, 2018, DOI: 10.1002/app.46215
ablationThreshold.context: Vulcanized natural rubber composite with 30% carbon black filler, 25°C, measured using 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser with 10 ns pulse duration under ambient conditions
